content:
Difference between endonuclease and exonuclease activity?
Are MMR enzymes always a part of the DNAP?
When, specifically does it choose to do endonuclease cleaving at the phosphodiesters vs exonuclease single bp?
How does UV Light, high energy radition, X-Rays, chemicals get into the body to modify nucleic acids?
What does oxidation, deamination, and alkylation of bases look like?
How does the base excision repair system rank?
Why is it called DNA glycosylase enzyme?
So you can divide the specific DNA repair pathways based on if damage was done to the double helix or not?
So the order is recognizing non double helix affected nucleotide damage, then a glycosylase will excise, then a endonuclease will cleave... so does the phosphodiester sugar backbone stay in tact when the glycosylase excises?
It seems the standard repair mecahnism once you have a clean slate is to put back on a DNAP and then a DNA ligase to rejoin the strands to seal nicks?
This is all in comparison to the nucleotide excision repair system (NER)?
Why are thymine dimers bad?
That distorts the double helix, thymine dimers?
Sometimes the chromsome itself, which Im not sure how do you define a chromsome?
Anyway, sometimes the chromosome itself has a double stranded break, what might cause a double stranded break, the same factors as before?

So the exonuclease activity removes from one end, the last base as opposed to endonuclease cuts inside the DNA strand, like a Restriction enzyme?
Doesn't DNA get synthesized 5' to 3', if so why is DNA polymerase's exonuclease activity opposing it, is it because it's reading at the end of the 5' to 3' strand which is 3', can't you just say that?
Ah I see so endo means interior and exo means exit from the end?
So MMR only happens after DNAP leaves?
DO DNAP's have endonuclease activity?
X-rays are very high energy photons, they can knock electrons off of atoms, can I have a visual example of this to store?
How do cigarette smoke and chemotherapy drugs contribute to DNA damage?Explain how breathing oxygen slowly damages DNA?
Oxidation is when Oxygen attacks bases, like nucleotide bases, can you show a visual of that?
Deamination is when you remove an amino group from a base like Cytosine which then becomes Uracil. Can you provide an image for that?
Show what a carbon-containing group being added on as in Alkylation means in an image?
Now take those past 4 questions and generate one image for it all?
So it goes BER and then NER?
What does AP mean for endonuclease? Does it mean apurinic or without purine or apyrimidic without pyridimine?
Glycosylase breaks the glycosidic bond, can you break down the word as to know why an ase breaks something, give another example?
Also I need an image for glyosylase, since it breaks at the sugar base junction?
Show the image of a thymine dimer? What amino acid causes kinks again, is it proline? maybe compare that?

What does endocytosis mean?
What is a DNA adduct?
What is benzo[a]pyrene?
Some chemotherapy drugs are topioisomerase inhibitors? Explain this to me with an example.
Expand more on ROS and I need an image for it. I know some examples are O2*, OH*, H2O2?
What does lesion mean?
Does 8-oxoG change Guanine to look like a different base?
Help me remember the Deamination image? Make it basic and memorable?
Challenge me to create my own image of oxidation, deamination, and alkylation on DNA?
Define lesion?
